past_medical_history: Generalized anxiety disorder, diagnosed at age 19
Major depressive disorder, two prior episodes at ages 22 and 25
Childhood history of emotional abuse per prior psychiatric notes

medications.indication: Generalized anxiety disorder and major depressive disorder

medications.frequency: as needed, up to twice daily

family_history: Maternal aunt with diagnosed conversion disorder in her 30s
No family history of epilepsy or neurodegenerative disease

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, approximately 1-2 drinks on weekends
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her partner in an apartment. Recently separated from her partner of 5 years approximately 3 weeks prior to admission.
social_history.exercise: Previously active, jogging 3 times per week. Unable to exercise for the past 2 weeks due to leg weakness.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. GCS 15. Speech fluent with normal rate and prosody. No dysarthria. Affect is mildly anxious but cooperative. Attention intact, able to recite months of the year backwards. Recall 3/3 at 5 minutes. No evidence of cognitive impairment.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive 3mm to 2mm. Fundoscopic exam normal, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements full, no nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in all divisions bilaterally. Masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no atrophy, no fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: Bulk and tone normal. Strength 5/5 bilaterally in all muscle groups. No pronator drift. Right lower extremity: Give-way weakness noted on formal strength testing. Initial contraction to 4/5 in hip flexors, knee extensors, and ankle dorsiflexors, followed by sudden collapse of effort. On distraction testing (e.g., when asked to lift contralateral leg), involuntary full strength is noted in the affected limb. Left lower extremity: Strength 5/5 throughout. Hoover sign POSITIVE on the right: involuntary hip extension on the right is strong when asked to flex the left hip against resistance, but voluntary right hip extension is weak.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the entire right leg, sharply demarcated at the inguinal crease with midline splitting on the trunk. Proprioception and vibration intact bilaterally in all extremities. Tubular visual field on confrontation testing of right eye noted.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose normal bilaterally. Heel-to-shin normal on the left. On the right, the patient reports difficulty initiating the movement but when performed, it is smooth and accurate. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient walks with a dragging right leg pattern that is inconsistent: the leg is dragged behind with the hip externally rotated, but when distracted (turning to look behind her), the gait briefly normalizes. No circumduction. Tandem gait not performed due to patient's report of imbalance, but she can stand on one leg (left) without difficulty.
neurological_exam.additional: Hoover sign positive on the right, strongly supporting functional weakness. Give-way pattern on manual muscle testing. Sensory examination shows non-anatomic distribution with exact midline splitting and sharp inguinal crease demarcation. During an observed clinical event on the floor (shaking episode lasting 2 minutes), the patient exhibited asynchronous, waxing and waning limb movements with side-to-side head shaking, eyes closed, and was verbally responsive during the event. No post-ictal confusion, no tongue biting, no incontinence.

patient.chief_complaint: Right leg weakness and episodes of shaking for two weeks
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Rachel Donahue is a 28-year-old right-handed woman and elementary school teacher who presents with a two-week history of progressive right leg weakness and recurrent episodes of whole-body shaking. The symptoms began abruptly approximately two weeks ago, shortly after she and her partner of five years separated. She was at work when she suddenly noticed her right leg felt heavy and she had difficulty walking. Over the following days the weakness persisted and she began experiencing episodes of generalized shaking. The episodes occur 2-3 times per day, lasting 1-3 minutes each. During these events she reports being aware of her surroundings but unable to control the movements. Witnesses have described the shaking as involving all four limbs with irregular, side-to-side head shaking and intermittent eye closure. There is no tongue biting, no urinary incontinence, and no post-event confusion. Between episodes she has persistent difficulty walking due to the right leg weakness. She has had to take medical leave from her teaching position. She was initially evaluated at an outside urgent care where a CT head was obtained and was reported as normal. She was placed on levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily empirically for suspected seizures but reports no change in the frequency or character of the episodes. She was referred for inpatient admission for continuous video-EEG monitoring and further workup. She endorses significant stress related to her recent separation and reports feeling overwhelmed by financial concerns. She has a history of anxiety and depression for which she takes sertraline. She denies any head trauma, fevers, recent infections, or travel. She denies any prior similar episodes, although she does recall a brief period in college where she experienced non-dermatomal numbness in her left arm during a particularly stressful exam period, which resolved spontaneously over several days.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-amplitude beta activity, symmetric, mildly increased likely secondary to anxiety or medication effect
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes in stage II sleep

artifacts.description: Prominent during clinical events due to patient movement, but underlying EEG discernible and remains normal

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. No significant change in background. No epileptiform discharges provoked. Did not trigger a clinical event.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal 48-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. Three habitual clinical events were captured, all showing NO EEG correlate. No interictal epileptiform abnormalities. No seizures recorded. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Movement artifact during clinical events partially obscured some channels, but sufficient channels remained interpretable to confidently exclude ictal EEG activity.

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No hyperintense lesions. No white matter signal abnormality.

additional_observations: Normal ventricular system and sulcal pattern for age
Pituitary gland normal in size and morphology
mri.impression: Normal MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium contrast. No structural lesion, demyelination, infarction, or mass to account for the patient's symptoms. Normal MRA of the head. Clinical correlation recommended.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized, reactive

output.impression: No epileptiform discharges or seizures were recorded. Clinical correlation recommended.

Motor_Nerve_Conduction.test: Right peroneal motor nerve (ankle to fibular head)
Motor_Nerve_Conduction.reference_range: Distal latency <6.5ms, CV >40 m/s, amplitude >2 mV
Distal latency <5.8ms, CV >40 m/s, amplitude >4 mV
Distal latency <4.4ms, CV >49 m/s, amplitude >4 mV

Needle_EMG.reference_range: No spontaneous activity, normal MUP morphology
No spontaneous activity, normal MUP morphology
No spontaneous activity, normal MUP morphology

signal_characteristics.T1: Normal signal intensity of the spinal cord throughout
signal_characteristics.T2: No intramedullary signal abnormality. No cord compression. Normal conus medullaris terminating at L1.
signal_characteristics.Post_contrast: No abnormal enhancement of the spinal cord or meninges

additional_observations: Normal cervical and thoracic vertebral body heights and alignment
No significant disc herniations or spinal canal stenosis
output.impression: Normal MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine with and without gadolinium contrast. No evidence of myelopathy, demyelinating disease, or structural abnormality to explain the patient's right leg weakness. Clinical correlation recommended.

Cognitive_Assessment.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test (total learning)

Psychological_Measures.reference_range: 0-4 minimal, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: PHQ-9 (Depression) 16 score (H), GAD-7 (Anxiety) 14 score (H), PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist) 38 score (H).

Psychiatric_Evaluation_Summary.value: Functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion disorder) with weakness and non-epileptic seizures; Major depressive disorder, recurrent, moderate; Generalized anxiety disorder; Probable PTSD related to childhood emotional abuse

Emotional conflict related to early attachment disruption and recent loss is expressed through somatic symptoms. The patient demonstrates alexithymia with difficulty identifying and expressing emotions verbally.

The patient demonstrates alexithymia with difficulty identifying and expressing emotions verbally.  (H).

results.title: Cognitive behavioural therapy for functional neurological disorder: A randomised controlled trial
results.summary: CBT specifically designed for FND (CBT-FND) reduced seizure frequency and improved psychosocial functioning compared to standard medical care in patients with PNES.

results.title: Physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms: A consensus recommendation
results.summary: Specialized physiotherapy focusing on movement retraining, distraction techniques, and graded activity is effective for functional motor symptoms including weakness and gait disorder.

results.title: Diagnosis and management of functional neurological disorder
results.summary: FND should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, entrainment) rather than by exclusion. Early diagnosis and explanation improve outcomes. Multidisciplinary treatment is recommended.

output.summary: Current evidence supports a multidisciplinary approach to FND treatment including psychoeducation, specialized physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms, CBT (particularly CBT-FND for PNES), and addressing psychiatric comorbidities. Diagnosis should be made positively based on clinical signs rather than by exclusion alone. Early diagnosis communication with empathic, non-dismissive language is critical for treatment engagement.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with right leg weakness and psychogenic non-epileptic seizures

differential.key_features: Events lack EEG correlate, asynchronous movements, preserved awareness, no post-ictal confusion, normal post-event prolactin. Video-EEG captured events with no ictal pattern.
Young woman with leg weakness could suggest MS, but normal brain and spine MRI, no oligoclonal bands expected, no dissemination in time and space, and positive functional signs on exam argue against.
Young patient with no vascular risk factors. No DWI restriction on MRI. Weakness does not follow vascular territory. Give-way pattern and Hoover sign inconsistent with upper motor neuron lesion.
Normal EMG/NCS, normal reflexes, non-dermatomal sensory distribution, give-way weakness pattern all argue against.
No fatigable weakness, no ptosis or diplopia, no bulbar symptoms. Give-way pattern is not the fatigable pattern seen in MG.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history focusing on symptom onset, precipitating stressors, event semiology, and psychiatric history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute onset of weakness with psychosocial stressor, episodes with features inconsistent with epileptic seizures, history of anxiety/depression and childhood trauma

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with specific testing for positive functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tremor entrainment, distractibility)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive Hoover sign, give-way weakness, non-anatomic sensory loss with midline splitting, inconsistent gait pattern that normalizes with distraction

optimal_actions.action: Order prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical events captured with no EEG correlate, confirming psychogenic non-epileptic seizures

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural pathology
Order basic laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, and post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All labs normal including post-event prolactin, supporting non-epileptic diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Communicate the diagnosis to the patient using empathic, positive framing (explain what the diagnosis IS, not just what it is not)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient understanding and engagement with the diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Taper and discontinue levetiracetam given confirmed non-epileptic events
Refer for specialized physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improvement in functional weakness with movement retraining

optimal_actions.action: Refer for CBT or neuropsychiatry for PNES management and comorbid psychiatric conditions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction in event frequency and improvement in mood/anxiety

optimal_actions.action: Consider EMG/NCS if clinical doubt remains about peripheral nervous system pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EMG/NCS confirming no peripheral nerve or muscle disease

optimal_actions.action: Order spine MRI if concern for myelopathy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal cervical and thoracic spine MRI

optimal_actions.action: Prescribe long-term antiepileptic drugs for the shaking episodes
Tell the patient symptoms are 'all in their head' or are malingered

critical_actions: Identify positive functional neurological signs on examination (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, distractibility)
Obtain prolonged video-EEG to capture habitual events and confirm absence of ictal EEG correlate
Diagnose FND based on positive clinical signs, not merely by exclusion of organic disease
Communicate the diagnosis empathically using patient-centered language
Refer for appropriate multidisciplinary treatment (physiotherapy, CBT/psychotherapy)
contraindicated_actions: Starting or continuing antiepileptic drugs for confirmed psychogenic non-epileptic seizures
Dismissing symptoms as malingering or fabricated
Ordering unnecessary invasive procedures (e.g., lumbar puncture, cerebral angiography) without clinical indication
Implying the symptoms are not real or that the patient is faking
Prescribing opioids or benzodiazepines as primary treatment for functional symptoms
key_reasoning_points: FND should be diagnosed by POSITIVE clinical signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, distractibility, inconsistency) rather than solely by exclusion
Positive Hoover sign (involuntary hip extension on the weak side when flexing the strong hip against resistance) is a hallmark examination finding for functional leg weakness
Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) are characterized by asynchronous movements, variable semiology, preserved awareness, absence of EEG correlate, and lack of post-ictal confusion
Normal post-event prolactin supports non-epileptic events (prolactin is typically elevated after generalized tonic-clonic seizures)
Recent psychosocial stressor (relationship separation) and psychiatric comorbidities (anxiety, depression, childhood trauma) are commonly associated predisposing and precipitating factors
Non-anatomic sensory distribution (exact midline splitting, sharp inguinal crease demarcation) supports functional rather than organic etiology
Treatment requires multidisciplinary approach: empathic diagnosis communication, specialized physiotherapy, CBT, and psychiatric treatment of comorbidities

metadata.teaching_points: This case demonstrates classic functional neurological disorder with both functional weakness (positive Hoover sign, give-way pattern) and psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (captured on video-EEG with no ictal correlate). The diagnosis is made by positive signs, not exclusion.
